overview: The war widened to a second sea overnight. As the US struck Iran for a tenth consecutive night and Iran's Revolutionary Guard hit two more ships in the Strait of Hormuz plus targets in Bahrain and Jordan, Yemen's Houthis escalated their Red Sea blockade — warning they will attack any tanker calling at a Saudi port and threatening to close a second strait — and President Trump vowed to 'take care of' them. Two chokepoints are now in play. The strain is showing at home: the Pentagon, the Washington Post reported, is 'quickly running short on cash,' and it admitted nearly 100 US troops wounded in two weeks atop 17 dead. Mediators pushed a 10-day ceasefire; Israel signaled it has 'no interest' in joining.

keyPoints.text: The US struck Iran for a tenth straight night to force Hormuz open; Iran's Revolutionary Guard said it hit two ships in the strait plus targets in Bahrain and Jordan.
Yemen's Houthis threatened to attack tankers calling at Saudi Red Sea ports and to blockade a second strait; Trump vowed to 'take care of' the group, opening a second chokepoint front.
The Pentagon is 'quickly running short on cash' after sinking billions into the war, the Washington Post reported, even as it admitted nearly 100 US troops wounded in two weeks atop 17 dead.
Mediators pushed a 10-day ceasefire as Trump 'eyes all-out war'; Israel's finance minister said Israel has 'no interest' in joining, though CNN reported it is readying contingency plans.

The military identified the soldier killed in Iraq as Sgt Michael Swinton; the WSJ reported an Iranian missile struck troop housing at Jordan's base, and Iran claimed an attack on Amazon infrastructure in Bahrain.
Brent crude held above $90 and up ~2.7% on the strikes and tanker fire; with pump prices past $4, Australia's central bank is now twice as likely to hike as fuel costs climb worldwide.

watch.text: Whether the mediators' proposed 10-day ceasefire gains any traction, or is overtaken by the all-out war the administration itself calls a 'time of ambiguity' and NBC now frames as a looming quagmire.

Whether the Houthis follow through on attacking Saudi-bound tankers and closing a second strait — and whether Trump's vow to 'take care of' them draws the US into striking Yemen, opening a formal second front.
Whether the bipartisan disquiet in Congress — Democrats hunting a mechanism to 'end it now,' some Republicans saying the war should have been 'finished' already — crystallizes into a War Powers vote.

stories.title: Tenth night of US strikes as Iran hits more ships in Hormuz
stories.summary: The war's central battle — over the Strait of Hormuz, the chokepoint through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil moves — remained a direct, tit-for-tat exchange through the day. The US struck Iran for a tenth consecutive night, and CENTCOM said the raids were aimed specifically at degrading Iran's ability to attack shipping, with explosions reported at Bandar Abbas, Qeshm Island, Chabahar and Konarak, the BBC and Al Jazeera reported. Iran answered in kind: its Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) said it hit two vessels in the strait — one tanker's crew forced to abandon ship, two tankers left ablaze, per the Guardian and AP — and struck targets in Bahrain and Jordan. Brent crude for September delivery rose about 2.7% and held above $90 a barrel, its highest of the war, with US average pump prices already above $4 a gallon. Washington is now bombing to force the strait open while Iran sinks and burns ships to keep it shut.
stories.explainer: A strait where vessels are actually being hit and abandoned is one insurers and shippers avoid regardless of any official 'closed' declaration — so the war's economic damage compounds with every ship struck, and there is no clean way to reopen a waterway both sides are shooting across. The knock-on is now reaching households far from the Gulf: with crude above $90 and pump prices past $4, Australia's Reserve Bank is judged twice as likely to raise interest rates, one small example of how a shooting match over one chokepoint feeds global inflation.

stories.title: Houthis widen Red Sea blockade; Trump vows to 'take care of' them
stories.summary: Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthi movement — the most active surviving member of Iran's 'Axis of Resistance' — sharpened its declared blockade of Saudi Arabia from a threat into an operational one, warning shipping companies that any tanker calling at a Saudi Arabian port on the Red Sea risks being attacked, the Guardian reported, and threatening to blockade a second key strait, per NPR. President Trump responded that the US would 'take care of' the group if it moved to block Saudi ships. The escalation opens a genuine second maritime front alongside Iran's closure of Hormuz: the Washington Post reported that a Red Sea blockade threatens a new front in the war, one that could severely curtail Saudi oil exports and force ships to choose between two contested waterways.
stories.explainer: Iran fights asymmetrically through allied militias, and the Houthis let it open a front it need not staff itself. A blockade is an act of economic war — threatening to interdict Saudi-bound ships raises insurance and freight costs across the Red Sea and pressures Riyadh the way Hormuz pressures the world. Two simultaneous chokepoint threats, Hormuz and the Red Sea, is the difference between a regional disruption and a systemic squeeze on Gulf energy. Trump's vow to 'take care of' the Houthis is significant because striking Yemen would formally add a third theatre — Iran, its Gulf allies, and now the Houthis — to an already overstretched US campaign.

stories.title: Pentagon 'quickly running short on cash' as war costs mount
stories.summary: The Pentagon, after sinking billions into the renewed Iran war, is 'quickly running short on cash,' the Washington Post reported — a rare admission that the material cost of a campaign fought largely from the air is outrunning the money appropriated for it. The disclosure lands atop a war the site's own figures put above $113 billion to US taxpayers as of June, and it arrives the same day the department acknowledged nearly 100 US service members wounded in two weeks. A funding crunch is a different kind of pressure than casualties or oil prices: it forces the administration to seek fresh appropriations from a Congress already searching for a way to end the war, handing lawmakers a lever over a campaign run largely on presidential authority.
stories.explainer: Wars are fought with appropriated dollars, and munitions, sorties, refueling and force deployments burn through them fast — a tenth straight night of strikes plus a global naval posture is expensive. When the Pentagon signals it is short on cash mid-war, it must eventually ask Congress for more, and that request is a vote: the same lawmakers hunting for a mechanism to halt the war would get to attach conditions, or refuse, giving the legislature a financial choke-point on a campaign it never formally authorized.

stories.title: Pentagon admits nearly 100 US troops wounded; Iraq soldier named
stories.summary: The Pentagon acknowledged that nearly 100 US service members have been injured over the past two weeks of escalating strikes on Iran, Time and the Guardian reported — a far larger toll than the official record had shown, disclosed a day after the New York Times reported the department had withheld dozens of injuries. The military identified the soldier killed in Iraq during a 'controlled detonation' of a downed Iranian drone as Sgt Michael Swinton of Fayetteville, North Carolina, bringing the confirmed US death toll to 17 (per ABC News and Time). The Wall Street Journal reported that the Iranian ballistic missile that killed two troops at Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase struck housing units for US personnel, and Iran separately claimed it had attacked Amazon infrastructure in Bahrain. The wounded are spread across Iraq, Jordan, Kuwait and Bahrain, the ring of US-aligned states absorbing Iranian reprisal fire.
stories.explainer: The injured, not just the dead, measure a war's true cost — and a count that jumps from 'dozens withheld' to nearly 100 in a day tells the public the toll has been understated. The specifics now emerging — a named soldier, a missile through troop housing, a claimed strike on cloud-computing infrastructure — turn abstract tallies into concrete vulnerability, feeding directly into the congressional push for a War Powers vote: if lawmakers conclude the administration hid the scale of American losses, the case to rein the campaign in strengthens.

stories.title: Mediators push a 10-day ceasefire as Trump 'eyes all-out war'
stories.summary: Regional mediators are pushing a specific 10-day ceasefire even as the fighting intensifies, CNBC and Axios reported, with US-mediated talks continuing through intermediaries alongside the tenth night of strikes. The effort runs against the current: Axios framed the moment as Trump 'eyeing all-out war,' NBC News warned the conflict threatens to become a 'quagmire,' and the administration itself called it a 'time of ambiguity.' On the widening question, Israel sent a mixed signal — Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich said Israel has 'no interest' in joining the renewed US campaign, easing fears of a second front, even as CNN reported Israel is quietly readying plans in case it must escalate. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio has signaled openness to talks and UN Secretary-General António Guterres continues to press for diplomacy, but with US troops dead and wounded and Tehran having called off the interim deal, the negotiating thread remains the war's thinnest — and only — off-ramp.
stories.explainer: With the interim deal dead and IAEA inspectors locked out, there is no standing framework to fall back on, so any de-escalation must be rebuilt from scratch through third parties — the role Oman and Qatar have played before. A 10-day ceasefire is a modest, time-boxed ask: not a settlement but a pause to let both sides step back from the chokepoints. Israel staying out matters because its re-entry is the fastest way to widen the war; Smotrich's 'no interest' lowers that risk even as CNN's reporting shows the option is being kept on the shelf.

stories.title: 'It needs to end now': a bipartisan search for the exit
stories.summary: Democratic lawmakers are actively searching for options to halt President Trump's renewed attacks on Iran — 'It needs to end now,' with the president accused of having 'lost control of this war' — turning last week's demand to terminate the war into a hunt for a concrete legislative lever, Politico and the Guardian reported. Senator Chris Murphy, the Connecticut Democrat, argued 'Iran knows Trump will have to stop escalation before it does.' The disquiet is not confined to one party: one House Republican said 'we should have finished this a long time ago,' and commentator Bill O'Reilly said Trump has 'shrinking options.' Cutting against a quick halt, The Hill reported the rising US death count is driving Trump toward escalation, casting the tenth night of strikes as reprisal 'in honour' of the fallen.
stories.explainer: Under the War Powers Resolution, Congress can force a floor vote to end US involvement in hostilities it never authorized — and 'searching for options' is the procedural step before such a vote is filed. Combined with the Pentagon's cash crunch, which will require fresh appropriations, the legislature is acquiring two levers at once: a war-powers vote and the power of the purse. The bind The Hill identifies — dead soldiers pushing the president to escalate while pushing Congress to quit — is exactly how past American wars have ended.

stories.title: Israel says 'no interest' in joining, but readies plans
stories.summary: Israel — which with the United States launched the war's opening blow, Operation Epic Fury, in late February — sent a deliberately mixed message about re-entering the renewed war. Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich said Israel has 'no interest' in joining the US military campaign against Iran, Al Jazeera reported, a public signal of restraint that eases the fastest route to a wider war. Yet CNN reported that on the sidelines of the conflict, Israel is quietly readying plans in case the situation escalates and it is drawn back in. Israel has stayed off the battlefield since the fighting reignited on 8 July, leaving the current phase a US-Iran duel over Hormuz, but has repeatedly warned it will respond 'with full force' if Iranian weapons strike its territory.
stories.explainer: Israel rejoining is the fastest way to widen the war, because it doubles the fronts Iran must fight and risks reactivating Hezbollah and other 'Axis of Resistance' militias along Israel's borders. Smotrich's 'no interest' and CNN's 'readying plans' are not contradictory: they describe a government that wants to stay out but is hedging against a single errant Iranian missile that could trip its 'full force' tripwire and force it back in.

stories.title: Washington 'teeters on all-out war,' NBC warns of quagmire
stories.summary: President Trump vowed Iran would 'pay many times over' for killing US soldiers while Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian declared his country in 'full-scale war,' as the Washington Post reported the US now 'teeters on all-out war' and NBC News warned Trump has entered 'an uncertain moment' that 'threatens to become a quagmire.' The tenth consecutive night of strikes was cast by Trump as reprisal 'in honour' of the fallen — a framing that narrows both leaders' room to stop, since any pause now reads as backing down. The maximalist language from both capitals marks the shift from a contained duel to a war each describes in total terms, with Iran returning fire across the Gulf and both sides hardening over Hormuz.
stories.explainer: When a US president and an Iranian president both reframe a limited campaign as 'reprisal many times over' and 'full-scale war,' they stake domestic credibility on out-hitting each other. That is how limited wars become total — not a single decision, but a ladder of matched blows neither side can be seen to leave unanswered. 'Quagmire' is the specific fear: a war with no defined victory condition that neither escalation nor talks can quickly close.

stories.title: US 'worldwide caution' as diplomats are left in limbo
stories.summary: The State Department issued a rare 'worldwide caution' security alert for US citizens abroad, the Guardian reported, warning of heightened risk everywhere as the war with Iran escalates — the kind of blanket advisory Washington reserves for moments when it expects reprisals against Americans far beyond the immediate theatre. The move came as NPR reported that hundreds of US diplomats have been left in limbo after the department drew down staffing at Gulf missions in response to Iran's attacks on host states, with no clarity on when normal operations resume. Together the steps show the war's reach extending past the battlefield into the machinery of American presence overseas.
stories.explainer: A 'worldwide caution' is broader than a country-specific travel warning — it signals the government judges the threat to Americans to be global, typically because it anticipates retaliation by Iran or its allied networks against soft targets outside the war zone. Paired with a diplomatic drawdown, it is a tacit admission that the conflict is no longer containable to the Gulf.

stories.title: The economics of a two-chokepoint blockade
stories.summary: Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthi movement's blockade of Saudi Arabia, first declared 20 July, hardened on 21 July into an explicit warning that tankers using Saudi Red Sea ports will be attacked, with the group also threatening to close a second strait, NPR and the Guardian reported. It stands as a second maritime front alongside Iran's closure of Hormuz, extending the squeeze from the Persian Gulf to the Red Sea and the approaches to Saudi ports. The Houthis spent the earlier phase of the war harassing shipping; a formal blockade of the region's largest oil producer widens the war's economic front and drags Riyadh, which has tried to stay on the sidelines, more directly into the fight.
stories.explainer: Two simultaneous blockades, Hormuz and the Red Sea, is the difference between a regional disruption and a systemic threat to Gulf energy flows. Even so, grist and other analysts note the war has not yet caused a global oil crisis — spare capacity, alternative pipelines such as the Iraq-Syria route, and strategic reserves have absorbed the shock so far. A second contested waterway shrinks that cushion.

stories.title: Oil holds above $90 as the shock ripples out to households
stories.summary: Brent crude for September delivery rose about 2.7% and held above $90 a barrel — its highest of the war — after the tenth night of US strikes and a fresh tanker fire in the Strait of Hormuz, the Guardian reported. US average gasoline remains above $4 a gallon and diesel above $5, and the shock is now visible in distant economies: experts warn the Reserve Bank of Australia is twice as likely to raise interest rates as higher fuel prices feed inflation, ASEAN diplomats voiced 'serious concern' over the energy crisis, and analysts urged looking 'beyond stocks' to gauge the real economic damage. With Hormuz a live shooting zone and the Houthis widening the Red Sea squeeze, markets are pricing sustained disruption.
stories.explainer: Roughly a fifth of the world's oil moves through Hormuz, so each vessel actually attacked strips the market of its assumption that some crude always slips through. The $4-a-gallon pump price is the transmission belt from a foreign war to inflation and central-bank decisions worldwide — from the RBA's rate calls to Southeast Asian import bills — which is why a 2.7% jump in Brent is a global macro event, not just a market one.

stories.title: Iraq soldier identified as Sgt Michael Swinton; 17 US dead
stories.summary: The US military identified the soldier killed in Iraq during a 'controlled detonation' of a downed Iranian drone as Sgt Michael Swinton of Fayetteville, North Carolina, the Guardian reported, bringing the confirmed number of US personnel killed in the war to 17 (Time published the full list). Not every US death has come from a direct hit — Swinton died disposing of ordnance — but the political effect is identical: each American killed raises the pressure on the president to answer. The deaths are spread across Jordan, Iraq, Kuwait and Bahrain, and the Wall Street Journal reported that the missile that killed two troops at Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase struck housing units for US personnel.
stories.explainer: A war spread across four host countries multiplies the ways US forces can be hit, and every death feeds both the reprisal spiral abroad and the revolt at home. Naming the fallen — and detailing that a missile reached troop housing — moves the toll from a running number to a set of concrete losses that lawmakers and the public weigh against the war's shrinking rationale.

stories.title: Iran calls off the interim deal, reviving the nuclear file
stories.summary: Tehran has hardened its formal rupture with Washington, calling off the interim peace agreement and warning of 'unforgettable lessons,' according to CNBC, after earlier declaring the June memorandum of understanding 'suspended.' The move reaches back to the nuclear file the framework had tried to freeze, and comes with IAEA safeguards access already suspended since strikes on Natanz. Cutting the other way, mediators are pressing for a new 10-day ceasefire and Marco Rubio has signaled openness to talks — but with US troops dead and nearly 100 wounded, the negotiating thread has frayed to near nothing.
stories.explainer: The interim deal was the last guardrail over the war's nuclear dimension: mutual commitments that, however frayed, gave both sides something to point to. Formally calling it off — not merely suspending commitments — removes that guardrail just as inspectors are locked out, leaving neither a live agreement nor independent monitoring over Iran's most sensitive facilities.

stories.title: Iran's reprisals keep falling on US Gulf allies
stories.summary: Iran's retaliation has continued to fall on the US-aligned states that ring it rather than on American soil directly, with fresh reprisals accompanying the tenth night of strikes, the Guardian reported. Iran said it struck targets in Bahrain and Jordan alongside the ships in Hormuz, and separately claimed it had attacked Amazon infrastructure in Bahrain — a rare claimed strike on commercial cloud-computing facilities. In recent days Kuwait and Bahrain have come under repeated missile and drone attacks, with a fire at a Kuwaiti electricity-and-water plant. The pattern matches Iran's threat, reported by The Hill, that if US strikes persist 'no political border' will be safe. A leaked US assessment concluded Iran retained roughly 70% of its ballistic missiles after the spring campaign.
stories.explainer: Hitting the US through the Gulf states that shelter its bases raises the cost of the American campaign while pressuring those governments to lean on Washington for a ceasefire. A claimed strike on cloud infrastructure widens the target set from bases and power plants to the digital backbone of the region — a way to make hosting American forces and companies politically and economically unbearable.

stories.title: The Iran war is America's least popular since polling began
stories.summary: The renewed Iran war is the least popular US conflict since modern polling began, The Economist reported, with public support falling below even the low points of the Iraq and Vietnam wars. The finding is driving events: it underpins the search for a way to terminate the war and sharpens what The Guardian calls Trump's 'midterm disaster' risk as American troops die — 17, per ABC News — and pump prices climb past $4 a gallon. Senator Mark Warner, the Virginia Democrat who vice-chairs the Senate Intelligence Committee, called it 'remarkable' there had not been more US deaths given how exposed American forces are.
stories.explainer: Domestic unpopularity is a strategic variable, not just a political one: a president fighting a war the public rejects has less room to escalate and more incentive to seek an exit — but dead soldiers pull the other way, creating pressure to 'finish' the fight rather than be seen abandoning it. That tension, now voiced openly on Capitol Hill, is exactly the bind Washington faces with midterms approaching.

stories.title: Nuclear dimension lingers at Bushehr
stories.summary: Satellite imagery published last week showed fresh damage inside Bushehr, Iran's largest nuclear power plant, from strikes dated between 7 and 12 July — the window in which the ceasefire collapsed — and Iranian officials have said US strikes also hit an under-construction nuclear power plant. With IAEA safeguards access suspended since strikes on Natanz earlier in the war, and Iran now calling off its interim-deal commitments, outside monitors are left reliant on satellites to gauge the condition of sensitive facilities. As US strikes push into Iran's south — Bushehr sits on the Gulf coast near targeted infrastructure — the risk of damage to a fueled reactor remains a background hazard.
stories.explainer: Bushehr is a civilian power reactor, not an enrichment site, so the danger near it is radiological rather than proliferation: damage to a fueled reactor's cooling or containment could release contamination across the Gulf regardless of who is blamed. With inspectors locked out, satellite analysis is the only independent window into whether the war is edging toward a nuclear-safety crisis.

notes: Evening refresh of the 2026-07-21 briefing. The manual RSS refresh via scripts/fetch-news.mjs was again blocked by the environment's egress policy (403 on all news hosts), so this draws on the workflow-committed feed (data/news.json, generated 2026-07-21T18:40Z; freshest item 18:36Z). Since the 11:08Z briefing the war widened to a second sea: Yemen's Houthis escalated their Red Sea blockade of Saudi Arabia — warning tankers calling at Saudi ports will be attacked and threatening a second strait — and Trump vowed to 'take care of' them, opening a second chokepoint front. The Washington Post reported the Pentagon is 'quickly running short on cash.' The military named the Iraq fatality as Sgt Michael Swinton (17th US death); the WSJ reported the Jordan missile hit troop housing; Iran claimed a strike on Amazon infrastructure in Bahrain. Mediators pushed a specific 10-day ceasefire; Israel's Smotrich said Israel has 'no interest' in joining while CNN reported it is readying contingency plans. Featured stories are ranked by systemic significance, scale, breadth, novelty and continuity; overview, keyPoints and stories describe the same events at different altitudes. Contested claims — casualty and injury figures, the 17-death tally and ~100-wounded count, the '70%' missile-retention estimate, oil and pump prices, the Pentagon funding shortfall, and Iranian- or Houthi-announced actions — are attributed to their sources.
